Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'm 26 and working part-time to make ends meet, so I couldn't always dedicate consistent blocks to studying. I'd usually try to listen to lecture recordings during commutes or while doing chores since I learn better by hearing things. When I did sit down to study, it was often late at night after work, which wasn't ideal but it's what I could manage with limited resources.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course materials because work picked up or bills needed sorting out. Then when deadlines loomed, I'd cram in as much as possible. I wished I could be more regular, but life at 26 with limited financial resources doesn't always allow for perfect scheduling.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder email or that creeping sense of panic that I was falling behind. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was something urgent I'd missed. Fear was honestly a big motivator for me—I'd get scared about failing and that would push me to at least check in. It wasn't the healthiest approach, but it kept me somewhat connected to the course.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural habit for me. I tried to make it routine a few times, like checking it every morning, but something always disrupted that pattern. Between work schedules changing and just feeling overwhelmed, I never got into a consistent groove with it. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped a bit with my anxiety, but they didn't really change my LMS habits.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely useful because I could listen to them multiple times. As an auditory learner, hearing the professor explain concepts worked way better for me than reading dense text. I'd often download the audio or play videos while doing other things. The few discussion posts I read were sometimes helpful too, seeing how other students understood the material.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. With limited internet at home, downloading everything wasn't always feasible, and staring at screens to read just didn't stick for me. The discussion forums I barely touched because I felt awkward posting—only did it once. Some of the optional quizzes I skipped because I was already stressed about the required work.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to passing the exams or completing assignments. If something seemed like it would directly help my grade, I'd prioritize it. Also, anything I could engage with through listening rather than reading felt more valuable to me personally. When I was already scared about falling behind, I couldn't afford to spend time on things that felt peripheral.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. When you're stressed about just completing the required stuff and managing work and life, optional things get pushed aside immediately. Required activities carried weight because failing them meant failing the course. I know optional stuff could have helped me learn better, but the fear of not finishing mandatory work always won out.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen on my phone during work breaks or commutes. I borrowed textbooks from the library since I couldn't afford to buy them. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos explaining the same concepts differently, which helped when I was confused. None of that showed up in any system.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't know. I'd replay downloaded lectures while cooking or cleaning. I'd sit with borrowed library books working through problems by hand. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with a friend from work who had some background in the subject. All that learning was real, but invisible to any tracking system.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd watch a video once online, then download the audio portion to listen to repeatedly offline. My internet isn't always reliable and I don't have unlimited data, so downloading things once and studying them multiple times offline was my main strategy. The LMS would only show that initial access, not the hours I spent with that material afterward.
Q12: Mid-semester got really rough. Work increased my hours unexpectedly, and I was already stressed about money. The anxiety built up and I felt scared about everything—failing the course, not making rent, all of it. There were a couple weeks where I could barely bring myself to look at course materials because it all felt too overwhelming. The mindfulness intervention helped a little, but the underlying problems didn't go away.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Confusion made me avoid certain topics because I didn't know where to start asking for help. Financial stress was constantly in the background, making it hard to focus. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open but be too mentally drained to actually engage with the content.
Q14: Yeah, this happened more than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered to worries about money or work. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials quickly just to feel like I was doing something, but nothing was sinking in. The fear of falling behind pushed me to access things, but being present and actually learning was a different story.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. Part of it was fear of doing them wrong—starting felt scary because then I'd have to confront what I didn't understand. Part of it was just being overwhelmed and avoiding the stress. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I had more energy, but tomorrow always brought its own problems. My assignment completion rate shows this pretty clearly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me into action. I'd see a deadline approaching and panic would set in, pushing me to finally engage. Most of my LMS activity probably clustered right before due dates. It wasn't a healthy pattern—I'd cram and stress and barely make it. Without deadlines, I might not have engaged at all during some weeks.
Q17: Feedback was mixed for me. When I got decent scores, it motivated me briefly. But when feedback pointed out problems, it sometimes made me more scared and avoidant rather than helping me improve. I didn't always check feedback promptly because I was anxious about what it might say. The delay in my response time probably reflects that hesitation.
Q18: The course structure was fairly flexible, which was both good and bad. Good because I could try to fit things around my work schedule. Bad because without rigid structure, I'd let things slide. Having 14 online courses to juggle meant I couldn't give each one proper attention. A more structured approach with clearer weekly expectations might have helped me stay on track better.
Q19: The reliance on reading materials made things harder for me as an auditory learner. When video content was available, engagement was easier. The discussion requirements felt awkward—I only posted once because I felt out of place among younger students. Having low resources meant some design assumptions about constant internet access didn't work for my situation. More audio options would have helped significantly.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my worst days—logging in out of fear, clicking around trying to feel productive, but being too anxious or confused to actually complete work. You might access everything trying to figure out what you need to do, but then get overwhelmed and not submit anything. High activity can sometimes mean high anxiety without productive output.
Q21: This partly describes me, actually. My exam score was decent even though my LMS engagement looked low. I did a lot of learning through downloaded materials and library resources that didn't show up anywhere. Someone might look at my login frequency and think I wasn't trying, but I was putting in work—just not in ways the system could see. Performance and visible activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and maybe patterns around deadlines. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether you actually understood anything, and all the offline studying. They can't capture that I listened to the same lecture five times while working. They don't know about the anxiety that made me avoid logging in, or the genuine learning happening away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through motions to pass. It's when your mind is present and you're connecting new information to what you already know. For me, real engagement happened during those moments listening to lectures and having things click, even if I was on a bus or doing dishes. It's not about where you are or what system tracks you—it's about genuine mental investment.
Q24: The LMS can't show how scared I was most of the time, or how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It doesn't capture the hours spent with borrowed textbooks or replayed audio files. It can't see that I'm a 26-year-old trying to better my situation with limited resources while working to survive. My engagement was real, even when it was invisible and even when fear made it inconsistent.
